The Albert Heijn Bonus Card Is Secretly a Loyalty Program (And You're Doing It Wrong)
Everyone has the little blue card. Almost nobody actually optimizes it. Here's how to squeeze real value out of AH Bonus, Air Miles, and the Koopzegels nobody talks about anymore.

Let's be honest: the Albert Heijn Bonus card is the single most-owned loyalty card in the Netherlands, and roughly 90% of people use it as a "make the price not insulting" button. You scan it, the Bonus discounts apply, you go home. End of story.

That's leaving value on the table. Let me bore you with the details.

## Bonus is a dynamic discount, not a static one

The Bonus folder rotates weekly, but the real trick is the **personal Bonus** offers in the AH app. These are algorithmically targeted to what you already buy. The hack isn't to buy more — it's to *time* your purchases of staples around when they hit personal Bonus.

> Rule of thumb: never buy coffee, olive oil, or laundry detergent at full price. One of them is always on a deep Bonus somewhere in a 3-week window.

## Stack with Air Miles

A lot of people forgot Air Miles still exists. It does, and AH is a partner. You earn miles passively, and the redemption catalog occasionally has genuinely good value — especially when you convert to specific retail vouchers rather than the "experiences" that quietly devalue your miles.

- Link your Air Miles to your Bonus card once and forget it
- Check the redemption rate before converting — it fluctuates
- Avoid the low-value impulse redemptions near the checkout

## Koopzegels: the boomer hack that still works

The savings stamps (*spaarzegels* / Koopzegels) give you roughly a small percentage back, and crucially you get a tiny interest-like bonus when you fill a full book. It's not life-changing, but for a household that already shops at AH weekly, it's free money for sticking labels in a booklet.

## The actual strategy

1. Always scan the Bonus card (obviously)
2. Buy non-perishable staples only when they're on Bonus
3. Keep Air Miles linked and redeem for vouchers, not fluff
4. Run Koopzegels if you're a weekly AH household
5. Use a cashback credit card on top for one more layer

Do all five and you've turned a discount card into a modest, stackable little money machine. Your friends will love hearing about it. Promise.

Black Friday in the Netherlands: I Tracked the Prices So You Don't Have To
Spoiler: a chunk of the 'deals' at Bol, Coolblue, and MediaMarkt are prices you could've gotten in October. Here's the data, the real bargains, and how to not get played.

Every November the Dutch retail internet loses its mind. Bol turns orange-er than usual, Coolblue sends you 14 emails, and MediaMarkt insists everything is a *megakorting*. So I did the boring thing: I tracked prices across the big NL retailers for several weeks before and after Black Friday.

The results are exactly as cynical as you'd expect.

## The "deal" that isn't

A meaningful share of Black Friday "discounts" were anchored to a **higher reference price** that the product hadn't actually sold at for months. Classic move:

1. Price drifts down over autumn
2. Reference price quietly resets to the old high
3. Black Friday "discount" brings it back to... roughly the October price

> EU rules require showing the lowest price from the prior 30 days. Read that small print — it tells you whether the deal is real.

## Where the real deals were

Not everything is a trick. The genuine bargains clustered in a few categories:

- **Open-box and refurbished** at Coolblue/Bol — frequently the best true value
- **Last-gen flagship electronics** being cleared for new models
- **Big-ticket appliances** where even a modest % is real money
- **Own-brand items** (Coolblue's own accessories, etc.) with actual cuts

## How to not get played

1. **Track before you buy.** Use a price-history tool; if the "deal" matches the 30-day low, it's fine — if not, walk away.
2. **Know the real price.** Decide what *you'd* happily pay before you see the banner.
3. **Ignore the countdown timer.** Artificial urgency is the whole business model.
4. **Stack cashback.** Layer a cashback portal or card on top of a genuine discount.
5. **Check return windows.** Many retailers extend returns into January for holiday gifting — useful leverage.

## The verdict

Black Friday in the Netherlands is neither a scam nor a goldmine. It's a **noisy market where a few real deals hide among a lot of theater**. Bring data, bring patience, and buy the thing you were going to buy anyway — just cheaper.

Or don't buy anything and feel smug. That's also a valid points-and-money strategy.

Dutch Taxes for Expats: The 30% Ruling, Box 3, and Other Things Nobody Warned You About
Moving to the Netherlands means meeting the Belastingdienst. A friendly-ish, mildly terrifying tour of the 30% ruling, the three boxes, and the deductions worth knowing.

Welcome to the Netherlands! The bikes are great, the bread is questionable, and the tax system is a labyrinth designed by people who really like boxes. Let's walk through the parts that actually affect your wallet.

## The 30% ruling (the famous one)

If you were recruited from abroad and meet the salary threshold, the **30% ruling** lets your employer pay up to 30% of your gross salary tax-free as a reimbursement for "extraterritorial costs."

- It has been getting steadily less generous — the duration and percentage have been trimmed in recent reforms, so **check the current rules for your start date**
- It also historically let you opt for *partial non-resident status*, simplifying Box 2 and Box 3 — another benefit that's been phased down
- You generally have a limited window to apply after starting work. Don't sleep on it.

> The 30% ruling is the single biggest financial reason a lot of skilled migrants come out ahead in the NL. Confirm your eligibility before you sign anything.

## The three boxes

Dutch income tax is split into three "boxes":

| Box | What's in it | Vibe |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Box 1 | Salary, home ownership | The big one |
| Box 2 | Substantial shareholdings (5%+) | Founders & directors |
| Box 3 | Savings & investments | The controversial one |

## Box 3 is where it gets weird

Box 3 taxes your *assets*, not your actual returns — based on a presumed (fictional) yield. This has been the subject of major legal challenges and ongoing reform because, surprise, taxing people on returns they didn't earn is unpopular.

If you have meaningful savings or investments, this is the box to get advice on. The rules are genuinely in flux.

## Deductions worth knowing

- **Mortgage interest** (*hypotheekrenteaftrek*) is still deductible in Box 1, though it's been gradually capped
- **Study costs**, certain **healthcare costs**, and **charitable donations** can be deductible
- Keep every annual statement (*jaaropgave*) — you'll want them at filing time

## The practical advice

1. Apply for the 30% ruling immediately if eligible
2. Register with DigiD so you can actually file
3. If you have assets, pay an accountant — Box 3 alone justifies it
4. File on time; the Belastingdienst is patient about almost nothing

It's not as scary as it looks. It's a *different* kind of scary. You'll be fine.

Flying Blue Promo Awards: How to Fly Out of Amsterdam for Almost Nothing
Every month KLM/Air France drop a fresh set of discounted Promo Rewards. If you live near Schiphol, this is the single best points play available to you. Here's how to actually win at it.

If you live in the Netherlands and you're not paying attention to **Flying Blue Promo Rewards**, you are leaving the easiest points value in Europe on the table. Schiphol is a KLM/Air France hub, which means award availability out of AMS is genuinely good — unlike basically everywhere else.

## What Promo Rewards actually are

Once a month, Flying Blue discounts a rotating list of routes by **25–50% off the normal mileage price**. The list changes on the 1st, the awards are valid for travel within a defined window, and the good ones disappear fast.

> A 25% off Promo Reward on a route you'd fly anyway is one of the highest-value redemptions in the entire program.

## How to win the monthly game

1. **Check on the 1st.** The new list drops at the start of the month. Set a calendar reminder.
2. **Search from AMS first.** Your home hub has the best availability. Don't overthink it.
3. **Be flexible on dates.** Promo award seats are capacity-controlled. Move your dates, not your destination.
4. **Watch the cash co-pay.** A "cheap" award with €180 in surcharges is not cheap. European short-haul usually has low fees; transatlantic on AF/KLM can sting.

## Earning the miles in the first place

You don't need to fly to rack up Flying Blue miles:

- **Amex Membership Rewards** transfers to Flying Blue 1:1 — this is the backbone of most NL points strategies
- **Bonus buys** during transfer bonuses (watch for 25–30% transfer promos)
- **Everyday spend** on a co-branded card if you fly KLM often enough to justify the fee

## The honest take

Flying Blue is not a "first class around the world for free" program. It's a *practical* program for people who actually live near a KLM hub and want cheap-ish flights to real destinations. Treat it like a discount engine, not a fantasy, and it pays off every single month.

Set the reminder. Check the 1st. Thank me later.
